text: The surgeon begins the gastrectomy by making a skin incision measuring 2.5 to 4 centimeters for the single incision port. The glove port is then inserted into the incision.

The surgeon inserts a needle passer with a suture below the sternum, penetrating the falciform ligament to retrieve the suture. The lesser sac is opened, and the suture is fixed to the gastrophrenic ligament.
A gauze is placed between the suture and the liver to prevent hepatic injury. The surgeon performs a partial omentectomy, opening the lesser sac and visualizing the posterior wall of the stomach.
The surgeon clears the greater curvature of the stomach just above the predicted resection site, using a bipolar energy device to ligate the left gastroepiploic vessels.
The surgeon performs a right side omentectomy, carefully dissecting the right side omentum while avoiding injury to the adjacent colon.

The surgeon identifies the duodenum and dissects the area around the number six lymph nodes near the right gastroepiploic vessels. The head of the pancreas is visualized, and the right gastroepiploic vessels above the pancreas are carefully dissected and ligated.

The surgeon clears the greater curvature of the stomach, ensuring sufficient space for delta anastomosis. A gauze is placed beneath the dissected pylorus to facilitate safe dissection. The lesser curvature is also cleared adequately for the anastomosis.

The surgeon identifies and ligates the right gastric artery. The pylorus is rotated ninety degrees to establish a transverse resection line, followed by the transection of the distal end of the stomach using a linear stapler.

The surgeon begins suprapancreatic lymph node dissection, focusing on the number eight lymph node area around the common hepatic artery. Dissection continues along the upper border of the pancreas and around the celiac artery, with noticeable lymph node enlargement. The procedure concludes with dissection above the common hepatic artery.
The surgeon dissects and ligates the coronary vein and the left gastric artery, while continuing to clear the lesser curvature of the stomach.
The surgeon ligates the anterior vagus nerve and dissects the number three lymph node area, carefully separating these structures from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon transects the stomach at the marked site and pulls the proximal stomach towards the duodenal stump to ensure there is no tension. A hole is created on the greater curvature of the proximal stomach, and another hole is made on the medial side of the duodenal stump. Sutures are placed on both the duodenal stump and the proximal stomach for traction purposes.

The surgeon inserts the 45mm cartridge side of the linear stapler into the hole on the proximal stomach, maintaining tension on the suture to prevent slippage. The stapler and stomach are pulled towards the duodenal stump, where the anvil side of the stapler is inserted into the hole on the duodenal stump. Both the proximal stomach and duodenal stump are everted, allowing their posterior surfaces to meet, and the stapler is fired to join the two structures.

The surgeon places tagging sutures on the superior and posterior anastomosis lines. A linear stapler with a sixty millimeter cartridge is used to close the entry hole, completing the delta anastomosis.
